text.title: Azure Compute Tier Selection Core Rules
text.summary: 6 testable rules every application of `Azure Compute Tier Selection` must enforce. Each rule pairs a binary check with a sourced rationale.

rule.statement: AKS workloads MUST use Workload Identity (OIDC federation); deprecated Pod Identity is forbidden.
rule.rationale: Pod Identity is deprecated; secrets-in-cluster patterns are higher-risk.

rule.statement: All Azure resource access from compute MUST use Managed Identity. Service principal credentials in env vars / secrets are forbidden.
rule.rationale: Stored credentials can be exfiltrated; MI tokens expire with the resource.

rule.statement: VMSS MUST use Flexible orchestration for new workloads; Uniform mode is deprecated.
rule.rationale: Uniform mode is being retired by Microsoft; Flexible supports mixed instance types and zone spreading.

rule.statement: Production VMs + VMSS MUST be deployed across Availability Zones (99.99% SLA). Availability Sets (99.95% SLA) are forbidden for new workloads.
rule.rationale: Zone failures, while rare, are documented; AZ deployment is the only zone-failure protection.

rule.statement: Pure Spot is forbidden for prod. Spot Priority Mix with a non-zero on-demand baseline is the only acceptable Spot pattern for prod.
rule.rationale: Spot eviction rates > 20% during peak demand make pure-Spot prod unreliable.

rule.statement: Compute resources MUST reach PaaS services (SQL / Storage / KV / ACR) via private endpoints, not public endpoints.
rule.rationale: Public endpoints expose PaaS to internet routing + DDoS even when locked down by firewall.

rule.statement: When preconditions in `Applies If` fail OR any item in `Skip If` triggers, do not run this methodology. Document the skip rationale in the artefact's parent log.
rule.rationale: Misapplied methodologies are worse than no methodology — they consume review cycles and produce false confidence.
